title: Ἀλώπηξ καὶ τράγος
source: Émile Chambry, Ésope: Fables, texte établi et traduit (Les Belles Lettres, Paris, 1927), fable 40 (= Perry 9). Text as printed, including Chambry's editorial punctuation (« », ⟨ ⟩ for editorial supplements). Pronunciation: reconstructed Attic, after W. S. Allen, Vox Graeca. Romanization: standard classical transliteration (ē/ō; th/ph/ch; y for υ, u in diphthongs; h = rough breathing; ēi/āi/ōi for iota subscript; accents omitted).

sentences.t: Ἀλώπηξ πεσοῦσα εἰς φρέαρ ὑπ' ἀνάγκης ἔμεινε.
sentences.tr: Alōpēx pesousa eis phrear hyp' anankēs emeine.
sentences.l: A fox, having-fallen into a well, of necessity stayed.
sentences.i: A fox fell into a well and had no choice but to stay there.
sentences.n: ὑπ' ἀνάγκης, 'under necessity' — ὑπό + genitive for the force acting on you. The fox's whole predicament is delivered in one aorist participle (πεσοῦσα) before the main verb: the fable engine again.

sentences.t: Τράγος δὲ δίψει συνεχόμενος ἐγένετο κατὰ τὸ αὐτὸ φρέαρ·
sentences.tr: Tragos de dipsei synechomenos egeneto kata to auto phrear:
sentences.l: And a he-goat, by-thirst gripped, came to the same well:
sentences.i: A goat, tormented by thirst, came along to the same well,

sentences.t: θεασάμενος δὲ αὐτὴν ἐπυνθάνετο εἰ καλόν ἐστι τὸ ὕδωρ·
sentences.tr: theasamenos de autēn epynthaneto ei kalon esti to hydōr:
sentences.l: and having-caught-sight-of her, he-inquired if good is the water:
sentences.i: and seeing her down there, asked whether the water was good.
sentences.n: ἐπυνθάνετο εἰ … ἐστι — an indirect question keeping the direct question's present tense ('is the water good?'), standard in Greek where English shifts to 'was.'

sentences.t: ἡ δὲ τὴν συντυχίαν ἀσμενισαμένη εἰς ἔπαινον τοῦ ὕδατος κατέτεινε, λέγουσα ὡς χρηστὸν εἴη τὸ ὕδωρ, καὶ καταβαίνειν αὐτὸν παρῄνει.
sentences.tr: hē de tēn syntychian asmenisamenē eis epainon tou hydatos kateteine, legousa hōs chrēston eiē to hydōr, kai katabainein auton parēinei.
sentences.l: And she, the encounter having-welcomed, into praise of the water stretched-out, saying that excellent was the water, and to-come-down him she-urged.
sentences.i: Delighted at the lucky meeting, she launched into praise of the water, saying how excellent it was, and urged him to come down.
sentences.n: εἴη is optative — the mood Greek uses for reported speech after a past-tense verb (λέγουσα … παρῄνει): 'saying that it was good.' You'll meet this optative of reported speech again. κατέτεινε 'stretched out (into praise)': she really laid it on.

sentences.t: Ἐπεὶ δὲ ἀμελετήτως κατῆλθε διὰ τὴν ἐπιθυμίαν, ἅμα τῷ τὴν δίψαν σβέσαι μετὰ τῆς ἀλώπεκος ἐσκόπει τὴν ἄνοδον.
sentences.tr: Epei de ameletētōs katēlthe dia tēn epithymian, hama tōi tēn dipsan sbesai meta tēs alōpekos eskopei tēn anodon.
sentences.l: And when heedlessly he-came-down because-of his desire, together with-the quenching of-the thirst, with the fox he-was-looking-for the way-up.
sentences.i: He plunged down without a thought, driven by his craving — and the moment his thirst was quenched, he and the fox were both hunting for the way out.
sentences.n: ἅμα τῷ … σβέσαι: the articular infinitive again — 'simultaneously with the quenching.' Greek nominalizes the action with τό + infinitive where English says 'as soon as he had drunk.'

sentences.t: Καὶ ἡ ἀλώπηξ ὑποτυχοῦσα εἶπε· « Χρήσιμον οἶδα, ἐὰν μόνον θελήσῃς τὴν ἀμφοτέρων σωτηρίαν.
sentences.tr: Kai hē alōpēx hypotychousa eipe: « Chrēsimon oida, ean monon thelēsēis tēn amphoterōn sōtērian.
sentences.l: And the fox, taking-up-the-word, said: « A useful-thing I-know, if only you-will the safety of-both.
sentences.i: The fox spoke up: « I know a way — if you really want us both to get out.
sentences.n: ἐάν + subjunctive (θελήσῃς): the ordinary future condition, 'if you (should) wish.'

sentences.t: Θέλησον οὖν τοὺς ἐμπροσθίους πόδας ἐρεῖσαι τῷ τοίχῳ, ὀρθῶσαι δὲ τὰ κέρατα, ἀναδραμοῦσα δὲ ἐγὼ καὶ σὲ ἀνασπάσω. »
sentences.tr: Thelēson oun tous emprosthious podas ereisai tōi toichōi, orthōsai de ta kerata, anadramousa de egō kai se anaspasō. »
sentences.l: Be-willing then the front feet to-plant against-the wall, and to-raise the horns, and having-run-up I will-pull-up you too. »
sentences.i: Just plant your front feet against the wall and hold your horns up straight; I'll run up over you, and then pull you out too. »

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ πρὸς τὴν παραίνεσιν αὐτῆς ἑτοίμως ἐπακούσαντος, ἡ ἀλώπηξ ἀναλομένη διὰ τῶν σκελῶν αὐτοῦ καὶ τῶν ὤμων καὶ τῶν κεράτων ἐπὶ τὸ στόμα τοῦ φρέατος εὑρέθη καὶ ἀνελθοῦσα ἀπηλλάττετο.
sentences.tr: Tou de pros tēn parainesin autēs hetoimōs epakousantos, hē alōpēx analomenē dia tōn skelōn autou kai tōn ōmōn kai tōn keratōn epi to stoma tou phreatos heurethē kai anelthousa apēllatteto.
sentences.l: And he to the advice of-her readily having-hearkened, the fox, having-leapt-up by-way-of the legs of-him and the shoulders and the horns, at the mouth of-the well was-found, and having-come-up she-was-going-off.
sentences.i: He readily did as she advised; the fox sprang up by his legs, his shoulders, and his horns, found herself at the well's mouth, climbed out — and started to walk away.
sentences.n: A genitive absolute opens (τοῦ … ἐπακούσαντος, 'he having complied'), then the escape runs through three genitives with διά: legs, shoulders, horns — the fox's staircase, item by item.

sentences.t: Τοῦ δὲ τράγου μεμφομένου αὐτὴν ὡς τὰς ὁμολογίας ἀθετήσασαν, ἐπιστραφεῖσα εἶπε τῷ τράγῳ· « Ὦ οὗτος, εἰ τοσαύτας φρένας εἶχες ὅσας ἐν τῷ πώγωνί σου τρίχας, οὐ πρότερον ⟨ἂν⟩ κατεβεβήκεις πρὶν τὴν ἄνοδον ἐσκέψω. »
sentences.tr: Tou de tragou memphomenou autēn hōs tas homologias athetēsasan, epistrapheisa eipe tōi tragōi: « Ō houtos, ei tosautas phrenas eiches hosas en tōi pōgōni sou trichas, ou proteron ⟨an⟩ katebebēkeis prin tēn anodon eskepsō. »
sentences.l: And the goat blaming her as the agreements having-broken, having-turned-round she-said to-the goat: « You there, if as-many wits you-had as in the beard of-you hairs, not sooner ⟨would⟩ you-have-gone-down before the way-up you-considered. »
sentences.i: When the goat reproached her for breaking their bargain, she turned round and told him: « Listen, friend — if you had as many brains as you have hairs in your beard, you'd never have gone down before you'd thought about the way up. »
sentences.n: A contrary-to-fact condition: εἰ + imperfect (εἶχες) with ἄν + pluperfect (κατεβεβήκεις) — 'if you had… you would not have…'. The ⟨ἂν⟩ in angle brackets is Chambry's editorial supplement: the manuscripts omit the particle the grammar wants. Ὦ οὗτος, literally 'O this-man-here!', is the Greek way of saying 'hey, you.'

sentences.t: Οὕτως καὶ τῶν ἀνθρώπων τοὺς φρονίμους δεῖ πρότερον τὰ τέλη τῶν πραγμάτων σκοπεῖν, εἶθ' οὕτως αὐτοῖς ἐγχειρεῖν.
sentences.tr: Houtōs kai tōn anthrōpōn tous phronimous dei proteron ta telē tōn pragmatōn skopein, eith' houtōs autois encheirein.
sentences.l: So also of men the sensible-ones must first the ends of affairs consider, then thus to-them put-hand.
sentences.i: So too a sensible man should first look to how a business will end, and only then put his hand to it.
sentences.n: δεῖ + accusative + infinitive: 'it is necessary for the sensible to consider…'. εἶθ' οὕτως, 'then, and only so' — the οὕτως resumes the condition just laid down. This moral's 'look before you leap' is literally 'consider the τέλη, the ends.'
